source.title: Toward a Fuller Integration of Respiratory Rhythms Into Research on Infant Vocal and Motor Development
source.venue: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences

confidence_rationale: Recent review in Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences; argues that canonical babbling represents the emergence of three-way coordination (motor, respiratory, vocal) not mere random vocalisation. The progressive coordination framing is relevant to the bootstrapping story. Source quality moderate-good (review, not primary data). Mapping fidelity good: the paper explicitly argues that babbling is a coordinated calibration milestone.
failure_signatures: The paper is a perspective/review with limited primary data; the mechanistic claim about respiratory rhythm as the coordination scaffold is proposed rather than experimentally demonstrated.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Canonical babbling is a multimodal motor milestone reflecting progressive coordination among respiratory, motor, and vocal subsystems; respiration provides the temporal scaffold for integrating speech articulation and movement.
mapping.ree_translation: Babbling is not noise but a phase in which multiple motor subsystems are being co-calibrated. This maps to the claim that a sufficiently broad and long exploration epoch is necessary to build the coordinated action store -- piecemeal exploration of one modality at a time leaves the multi-modal behavioural repertoire incomplete.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The three-way respiratory-motor-vocal coordination is specific to speech production; REE's action repertoire involves spatial navigation rather than vocalization.
mapping.source_context: Review synthesising motor control, physiology, speech, and language acquisition literatures; Annals NYAS, 2026.

confidence_components.notes: Good conceptual fit; primary caveat is review-only status and vocal-specific framing.
